case_name: Debbie Pyzyk, Plaintiff/Respondent v. Gateway Psychiatric Group, LLC & Gordon Robinson, M.D.

summary.brief: Debbie Pyzyk, Plaintiff/Respondent v. Gateway Psychiatric Group, LLC & Gordon Robinson, M.D.. Medical malpractice case involving oncology.

evidence_index.text: Dr. Gordon Robinson is a psychiatrist who began treating K.P. on June 25, 2013.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: K.P. suffered from various health conditions, including alcohol dependence, ADHD, amphetamine abuse, generalized anxiety disorder, bulimia nervosa, and anorexia nervosa.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Robinson prescribed Vyvanse (Lizdexamfetamine), 1 an amphetamine.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Initially, Dr. Robinson prescribed one dose of 50 mg per day. Dr. Robinson changed the prescription to two doses of 50 mg (a total of 100 mg) per day.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Following the increase in dosage, K.P. lost weight and developed a picking habit.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: K.P. was abusing the Vyvanse and taking more than the prescribed amount.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: K.P.’s cause of death was an “intraventricular hemorrhage secondary to acute Lisdexamfetamine toxicity.”
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Plaintiff’s standard of care expert identified as outside the standard of care Dr. Robinson’s: prescription of Vyvanse in excess of the FDA’s
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiff's expert

evidence_index.text: We affirm the judgment of the trial court.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The jury rendered a verdict in favor of Plaintiff and awarded $5 million in compensatory damages, which the trial court reduced to $801,061 pursuant to the medical malpractice liability limitations in Section 538.210.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

patient_demographics.relevant_social_history: History of alcohol dependence and amphetamine abuse

initial_state.chief_complaint: Seeking treatment for ADHD and alcohol dependence
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: K.P. was admitted to Harris House Treatment and Recovery Center for inpatient treatment of alcohol dependence and Adderall abuse. She had a history of abusing Adderall to continue drinking and to keep weight off.

focused_exam.general: Weight loss and picking habit developed after Vyvanse dosage increase

requestables.name: Drug Screen
requestables.description: Screen for amphetamines and other substances

reveal.clinical_significance: Indicates Vyvanse use and potential abuse
reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt reassessment of medication management

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient admitted to Harris House for treatment

key_events.event: Vyvanse prescription increased to 100 mg per day

timeline_phases.name: Follow-up and Monitoring
timeline_phases.description: Monitoring of patient's response to medication

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with history of amphetamine abuse and current Vyvanse prescription
decision_points.prompt: What should be the approach to prescribing Vyvanse?

options.description: Prescribe Vyvanse at FDA recommended maximum dosage of 70 mg per day
options.clinical_reasoning: Adhering to FDA guidelines minimizes risk of abuse and toxicity

options.description: Prescribe Vyvanse at 100 mg per day
options.clinical_reasoning: Higher dosage increases risk of abuse and toxicity

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant prescribed Vyvanse at 100 mg per day
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Effect of Vyvanse was wearing off in the afternoon

expected_action_court.description: Vyvanse should have been prescribed at or below 70 mg per day

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Prescribing above FDA recommended dosage increased risk of toxicity
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Adherence to FDA guidelines and closer monitoring

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient on Vyvanse with signs of abuse
decision_points.prompt: How should the patient's medication management be adjusted?

options.description: Continue current Vyvanse prescription without changes
options.clinical_reasoning: Patient was not seen regularly, and signs of abuse were not addressed

options.description: Discontinue Vyvanse and consider alternative ADHD management
options.clinical_reasoning: Signs of abuse and potential toxicity require reevaluation of treatment

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant continued Vyvanse prescription without changes

expected_action_court.description: Vyvanse should have been discontinued or adjusted
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony on managing amphetamine abuse

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to adjust treatment despite signs of abuse

patient_outcome.description: Patient suffered an intraventricular hemorrhage and was declared brain dead

key_findings: Breach of duty in prescribing Vyvanse above recommended dosage
Failure to monitor and adjust treatment despite signs of abuse

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to adhere to FDA guidelines and monitor patient
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Adherence to guidelines and monitoring could have prevented toxicity

diagnoses.diagnosis: Intraventricular hemorrhage secondary to acute Lisdexamfetamine toxicity
